ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried celery with beef; mapped to the broader stir-fried food product class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the primary protein anchor for this mixed stir-fried dish containing beef and celery
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No single FoodEx2 code covers the composite dish; A0CKP (cooked beef) is used as the closest parent ingredient anchor. Celery component maps to FoodEx2 A00LQ (celery) but the composite dish code is absent from FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried celery with beef as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried celery with beef as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, representing one shared-plate portion alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Celery with Beef — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Celery with Beef (cr_id F14080044). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and celery composition data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetable and lean beef composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried celery with beef. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for non-starchy vegetables (GI ~15) and cooked beef (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution). The dish carbohydrate content is 2.5 g/100g, predominantly from celery and trace cornstarch. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6. This is a very low GL, consistent with a non-starchy vegetable and protein-dominant dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 394 mg/100g — borderline high; potassium ~280 mg/100g from celery and beef provides partial offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120 g and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce preparation to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per serving; the potassium content from celery provides a modest but meaningful natriuretic offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.5 g/100g) and low sugar (1.5 g/100g); high protein (12.4 g/100g) from beef slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load (estimated GL 0.6 per 150 g serving) and high protein content; trace cornstarch in the marinade is not a clinically significant concern at typical serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100g from beef; sodium at 394 mg/100g; red meat consumption is associated with elevated cardiovascular risk at high intake frequency
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish occasionally in moderate portions (100–120 g) while requesting lower-sodium seasoning; substituting lean beef cuts or reducing beef quantity can lower saturated fat and sodium exposure, and celery's phthalide compounds may provide a mild vasodilatory benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from beef; substituting beef with chicken breast or tofu and retaining the celery and aromatics is recommended to preserve anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefits while eliminating the red meat concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Celery with Beef (西芹炒牛肉) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-cooked and hawker-style dish consisting of thinly sliced beef marinated in soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and cornstarch, then wok-fried at high heat with celery stalks, garlic, and ginger until fragrant and just tender. The dish delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a satisfying contrast between the crisp-tender celery and the tender, well-seasoned beef. Per 100 g, it provides 130 kcal, 12.4 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option suitable for weight management and blood glucose control. Sodium is 394 mg/100g, which is borderline and warrants portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. Clinically, it is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low glycemic load (estimated GL 0.6 per 150 g serving) and high protein content. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. Celery contributes dietary fibre (1.2 g/100g), potassium (~280 mg/100g), and anti-inflammatory polyphenols including apigenin and luteolin. This dish is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice in Singaporean Chinese family meals and zi char restaurants.

Hawker centre mixed rice (cai fan) topping

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080044) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and celery composition data. Potassium estimated from celery and beef USDA reference values. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous non-starchy vegetable and beef values. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F14080044, and no equivalent composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef (lean, cooked) and celery composition data proportional to the dish composition; this is an approximation. 3. Potassium (280 mg/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central values for celery (~260 mg/100g) and lean beef (~318 mg/100g) weighted by approximate dish composition; not directly reported by HPB. 4. Input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a beef-containing dish and have been removed. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable reference values; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the published literature. 6. Halal status retained from input; beef sourced from halal-certified suppliers in Singapore hawker context is standard but should be verified at point of purchase. 7. FoodEx2 match type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the permitted enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] required by VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md.
